description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 13: Acclimatising in lunar orbit

tokens: Everyone here is watching displays and listening.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We don't have any voice communication yet, but at the time we got data indicates that was a very good burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You can tell the world that we have arrived.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's good to hear from you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, you wouldn't believe this thing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes, the guidance was absolutely fantastic, and we'll give you the - the burns right now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, my hat's off to the guys in the Trench.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes, kiss that man that runs MSFN.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't know whether I can do that, though, but I'll say thank you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You ready to copy our burns?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You ready to copy the postburn report?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Our roll, pitch, and yaw, and angles guidance was all good.
The fuel remaining is 37.7 percent, oxidizer is 39.5 percent.
The unbalance - I'd like to talk about - The present unbalance is 500 increase.
The chamber pressure increased smoothly throughout the burn from 98 to 103 with no apparent discernible jump at second ball-valve initiation.
It bounced up to 98 and then smoothly from 98 on to 103 with all four ball valves on.
The unbalance - Are you still with me?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I watched the unbalance go from where we left it at 200 decrease from the short burns.
I didn't touch it until after everything settled down after we were into the burn for 30 minutes.
At that time, the unbalance was 300 to 350 decrease.
I put the oxidizer flow valve in a Decrease position.
I brought it up to zero.
I closed it and then it started on up.
After it hit about 150 increase, I put it to Increase and it held it, barely held it, and it creeped up from like about 250 to the present point at 500.
I actually neutralized the flow valve at about 20 seconds before the burn ended and when I put it to Normal, then the increase went from about 400 to 500.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is advising MCC-H about how busy he had been with the propellant balancing systems during the LOI-1 burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Note that the timings given in this conversation differ from those in the technical transcript.
A suspected error in the minutes figure has been adjusted to make more sense.
If unedited audio becomes available, the timings will be revisited.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It looks like you really have arrived.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And the oxidizer, the oxidizer and fuel remaining agree very accurately with the onboard graph I have of the helium pressure, which is about 1750 right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And the first view I had of the Moon was reflected in the overhead window of the LM.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's Gene Cernan with the post burn report.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: During LOI, the big SPS engine on the aft end of the Service Module is facing the direction of travel which means that the CM windows are facing generally rearwards with the LM in view.
As they came towards the point where the burn would occur, they were in the Moon's shadow.
The LM's overhead window, on the other hand, is facing forwards and is in Gene's field of view from his right-hand seat.
As they neared the Moon's sunset terminator, Gene could see the obliquely-lit landscape reflected in this window.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You'd have to see this planet to believe it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've got FIDO looking at your - the radar residuals are very small.
Give us a chance to track awhile, and we'll confirm.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You mean - you don't think it's - I think it's confirmed, as far as I'm concerned.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's John Young that's convinced there.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have our student geologists here overlooking the surface, and they'll report in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're just passing from the highlands over into the Mare area, and you can pass on to Jack we caught a couple of real pretty little volcanoes, there's no doubt about them, and we got a couple of good high-resolution photos, and it still looks kind of brownish-gray to us here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: There were some places back there where...
There was one volcano, whatever it was, that - It was all white on the outside but definitely black around the top of it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie, it might sound corny, but the view is really out of this world.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Laughter) We had a couple of comments (laughter) from the back row that I won't repeat.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Those first words we have received were from Tom Stafford and John Young commented and then Gene Cernan gave the post-burn report.
That was also Tom with reporting two volcanos and then John Young came back in with the description of what looked like a volcano black around the top.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have a map update for Rev 2 if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got a sunrise time of 77:51:40 and a sunset of 79:13:33.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The map update times are given for each revolution and are hand written on the Flight Plan.
The times given for Rev 2 are LOS, 77:47:59; sunrise, 77:51:40; crossing 150°W, 77:58:27; AOS, 78:31:19; sunset, 79:13:33.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: According to our maps, we have you coming up on the Sea of Waves and to your left Langrenus.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 76 hours, 34 minutes and as you've heard, Gene Cernan reported that burn was right on time.
And the murmur of almost unbelievable ran through this Control Center when they read out those residuals.
The onboard reading of the orbit - lunar orbit for Apollo 10 was 169.1 nautical miles at the burn, 59.6 nautical miles [313.2 by 110.4 km] pericynthion.
We were shooting for 60 by 170 [111 by 315 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Man, we could see the Sea of Crises up here.
That's the first real thing that I'm positive of that I've seen that I recognize and boy, it really stands out.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That was John Young reporting he could see the Sea of Crises - stands out very well.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: One thing about the orbital rate up here in the track, it's considerably slower than around the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And, also, looking out at the horizon, some of the mountains we can see down here - That's going to be a real kick tomorrow down at 50,000 feet [15,000 metres].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 10, we're expecting an appropriate comment tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This will be our VOX, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And the people in the trench that the crew showered the praise on are the Flight Dynamics Officers whose consoles are on the front row in the control center.
That area is referred to as the Trench.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And FIDO wants to get some tracking before he commits himself on the orbital parameters, but he expects them to be right on nominal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've got Langrenus, now, out here off the - It depends on which way you roll, but off to the one side here.
Very beautiful sharp peak right in the center.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And it appears our water boiler is working, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We picked it up a moment ago.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The primary evaporator (boiler) which had dried out during the launch ascent, was reserviced and operated during the LOI-1 burn and continued to be used until it again dried out during the second lunar revolution.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are working on a time for you to cross to Site 1, and did you attempt to call a second ago?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We are showing spacecraft weight now at 69,493 pounds [31,594 kg].
Apollo 10 losing a lot of weight in that long LOI burn.
The weight at LOS was 93,281 pounds [42,408 kg].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have a time crossing latitude for - correction, longitude for Site 1: 76:49:00.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And I think we are coming over the Taruntius Twins now.
Looks like we've got them real clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: What is your early estimate of landmark tracking ability?
Did Jack do a good job for you?
You got a lot of good landmarks?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Starting to look just like NASA Road 1 out there now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: At the speed we are traveling, that TV camera with the zoom should really give you a fantastic picture.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: NASA Road 1 is the road in front of the Manned Spacecraft Center.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You will be glad to know we are walking right up our LM chart, right up our track in the Apollo Ridge, right now.
We've just seen the four Taruntius: Papa, Kilo, Hotel and George.
We've seen big Taruntius; we're looking at Messier and Messier A; and Secchi K ought to be coming up, and then Secchi.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are following along with you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That was Gene Cernan calling out those landmarks along the ground track.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're B-1 right now, come to think of it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Secchi is very well defined as we come into Apollo Ridge.
The rille perpendicular to the ridge and parallel to the ridge is very well defined in this area.
The chicken-track area is very easy to see from this altitude.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As you near Site 1, if you get a chance, could you comment on the volcanic cones and the highlands south of track?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're right over Censorinus at this time, at least through my hatch window.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And I've got the terminator out my window, coming up.
It sure makes the landscape look a little different.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Could you comment on the shadows as you come up to the terminator and the - your ability to detect landmarks in that area?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think it's going to be real good.
Just like the "8" guys said, you can see down into these shadows.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Like, I'm looking at - down at one crater and there's a crater that's underneath the shadow, but I'm not having any trouble at all seeing it from here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've reached 208 inertial, and we'll just hold their attitude around since it's the same attitude as per Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The Flight Plan calls for the attitude to be held at an inertial attitude of 208° pitch, 135° roll and 0° yaw.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, there is no doubt about it.
This maria area out here is darker than the other.
It looks like it's turning nearly black, where before looking out there, it's like a light shade of grayish-brown.
And I bet that TV camera will show it pretty good.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're standing by for the TV, and stopped the orb-rate at 208 inertial.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The ORDEAL panel 13, FDAI 1 & 2 switches, have now been moved from the Orb Rate position to the Inrtl (inertial) position to enable the spacecraft attitude to be maintained at 208° pitch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Charlie, I got Theophilus right on the terminator here, and you can see well down into it.
It's got a very pronounced central peak which is nearly as high as the rim, and it's got a little rim crater just on the inside which is very easily distinguishable.
And then just preceding it at 30 east and about 11 south, the small crater preceding it in contrast has no central peak that's visible.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've located you on a map.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, I'm right over Maskelyne and Maskelyne B now to be leading right into Landing Site 2.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've - keeping a cheek on all your systems.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And Sidewinder Rille and Diamondback Rille stand out just tremendous here.
We're just about to cross the terminator.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Boy, that's really something there, I don't see why fish aren't dumped down that creek.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And Torricelli is off to the right on my forward window, very easily distinguishable at this Sun angle.
Those rilles are something else again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll have you coming up to Site 2 at 76:53.
You might be in there too dark at that point, but that's the time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: At 208, Houston, here, inertial attitude, we're going just about straight down.
And most of the terrain right down below my window is starting to disappear and nighttime - it's getting black here, but the one thing that really stands out was those features that we picked out.
And I guess a11 the homework has paid off because, like I said, it's just like NASA Road 1 leading up to it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got our friendly geologist back here grinning, and looks like we're going to be Go far all the landmark tracking and everything, then.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And I've just picked up Moltke down below.
I can just see a little bit of a white rim and the rest is black.
Landing Site 1 - pardon me, Site 2 is completely in the black, but I can see half of the rim of Moltke, and that's about it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Although we're going into this backward, it's still amazingly easy to pick up these landmarks as we're going into the landing site.
Especially the ones that we've worked on a lot - a lot more heavily.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you have your - are you monitoring...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We should be looking down, of course, this isn't - We're looking down right now as Tom said, right over Site 2.
It's in darkness, and we've got a lot of reflected Sun off the LM, but right over in the Surveyor V area, also, but it's in darkness at the present time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Also, you can - The feature we called "U.S.
It disappears in the darkness right by Moltke, and the area over to the right.
There's no doubt there's been some volcanism in there and that's what we term the Oklahoma Hills.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We thought you had your descent strip chart out.
We're breaking ours out here now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I knew he'd name something, "Oklahoma Hills."
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: CDR-Thomas Stafford was born in Weatherford, Oklahoma.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You notice he got that out on the first rev, too.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, Theophilus is still visible out my side window.
Theophilus is still visible out my side window and it's right on the terminator, and it's beautiful the way the shadows are falling on it.
The central peak looks like it's going to last just about as long in sunlight as the far rim.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like you to elaborate a little bit on your - the rilles that you commented on about 5 minutes ago: Diamondback and Sidewinder.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll tell you, from my experience around the Earth, you can tell Jack it looks like Canyon Diablo out there in New Mexico.
They're definitely dropped down with sharp walls.
It doesn't look like there is any build-up along the sides.
It's just straight down like a graben at least from this angle up here - At least for 60 miles, it looks like they're straight down and it kind of looks something like Canyon Diablo.
And we'll give you a better description tomorrow at 50,000.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 1" looks like it's got pretty vertical edges, but again, this is from 60 miles [111 km].
We'll give you a better description tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: In the rilles, can you see - Do you think you can see the bottom of those things?
Do you see any boulders or anything down there?
It's probably pretty difficult from that altitude, but can you comment on that?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sixty miles is too far up.
It's mostly dark down there at this sun angle.
Later on, when we see some around on the other side and tomorrow, we'll give you a better description.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: To tell you the truth, I didn't look that close.
The shadow that goes down in there - it - all you can tell was the rille.
You couldn't see the bottom of it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Gene-o says that the ones he looked at were rounded off in the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You might tell Jack that he forgot to tell us to practice studying these landmarks standing on our head.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We - He heard the comment, and we'll take care of that for the next flight.
We got a - We'll have no update for you on your block data for the TEIs, and we confirm your abort.
Your orbit is 60.6 by 170.1 [112.2 x 315.0 km] on 8 minutes of tracking.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I guess we owe you, don't we?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You want me to leave my high bit rate switch in High?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like your bit rate switch to go to Low.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sorry, I didn't catch that earlier.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is referring to the PCM (Pulse Code Modulation) bit rate switch on panel 3.
The function of the PCM TLM equipment is to convert telemetry data inputs from various sources into one serial digital output signal.
This signal is either transmitted to Earth, or recorded on the DSE for later transmission.
Switching it to the Low bit-rate mode of 1.6 kbps, allows high PCM bit-rate of 51.6 kbps to be commanded by MCC-H via MSFN on demand.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I guess I'm looking for some words on - on the PUG switch as to whether or not you want me to go ahead and put this on in Increase at the start of the next burn or possibly use Secondary, considering the unbalance we've got.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene is still pursuing the SPS propellant balancing.
He is now asking whether they should switch to the Secondary-Oxid Flow Valve, rather than the Primary valve used during LOI-1.
The Secondary valve would normally only be used if the Primary has been deemed to have failed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll get you some words on that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The oxidizer flow valve is what I'm referring to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll get you some words in a minute, 10, Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The thing I didn't understand about it was I waited - waited until it settled down.
I brought it back up very smoothly just before zero, and I tried to lead it, and I closed it.
And then she started going up, and I started it to the Increase position at 150, and then I could barely hold my own.
And in fact, I was losing ground the whole time.
I did see it go through the cross-over point through the 57-percent regime down to about 51 or so, and she did fluctuate all over the place, and then settled down again afterwards.
And I left the oxidizer flow valve in the Increase position throughout that whole part of that burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It'll take our G&C guys a while to analyze the tapes.
We'll give you some word after - on our next rev.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Gene is asking for advice on how to operate the SPS propellant balancing system for the upcoming LOI-2 burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I never saw nothing like that.
We - when we came - When we came around on the back side, seems like the colors are different on the back side - more light than they are on the front side.
I wouldn't say it's - I wouldn't say it's - It's shades of black and white and browns in there, and near as I can tell, there's some brown in that thing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There are all kinds of shades of gray, of colors.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Copy, on the back side that the colors are different, that it appears to be more browns and blacks.
Is that correct, in the maria?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think it's different from the maria.

One thing that really stands out that wasn't impressed on me before we got here is that - is a very great observable difference between - between the - far as elevation is concerned, between the mare and the surrounding terrain - the surrounding highlands.
Boy this is really a rugged planet.
And I saw a big basin on the back side, and we'll have to, get around there and look at it again.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It ought to be coming up in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got a beautiful view of the Earth here, and pardon, the Moon in Earthshine.
Sorry, about that slip, but it's absolutely fantastic here at night with Earthshine.
Our TV camera might have enough to pick that up, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, the craters - The center of some of the lighter craters glow as if they're lit by radioactive - They just glow in this very low, dim light.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In about 10 minutes you cross 45 west, look directly north and see if you can see the crater Aristarchus.
It's near the horizon - the northern horizon.
There have been some reports last night and the night before of some transient events in that crater - some glowing, and they were hoping that you might be able to give them some word on that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got it located, And that's in the Ocean of Storms about 40 - maybe 47 west and about 23 north?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's going to be pretty close to the northern horizon, but you might be able to see it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I've got Copernicus by Moon - by Earthlight.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Apollo 10 is approaching 40 degrees west, now, getting near landing site No.
5 which will be north of the ground track and the landing area for Surveyor 1 is south of the ground track of Apollo 10.
10 should pass just a little bit north of Surveyor 1 before too long.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're set up in this sleep configuration right now, as far as the roll, pitch, and yaw goes, and we've got it in wide deadband.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In 10-degree deadband, plus or minus 10 degrees.
That's all we are allowed in this sleep configuration, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You've got the proper entry in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We were not able to see any particular activity in the area of Aristarchus.
It's amazing, though, how well you can - once you find the landmark, navigate in Earthshine across the surface of the Moon.
It seems to be very well lit from our altitude here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are hoping that we can get some TV past the terminator.
The TV experts are looking at it, and we think that we might be able to get some.
We will let you know next time around.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The Moon past the terminator is totally dark as long as we are in sunlight, but the minute we go out of sunlight, in the darkness ourselves, the Moon then glows right at us.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I can - The LM thrusters stick out like a sore thumb in Earthshine, too, but they don't keep us from seeing any of the stars up here at night.
It is real well lit up.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: In your P52 you can recognize everything, and no problem.
That was not blocked by the LM.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's right, and, thus far, believe it or not, we haven't run anything where the LM blocked us from a star.
There was one case, but so far we have been lucky.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: In Earthshine, you can see way down in the craters.
You can see the shadows in the craters from the Earthshine, but the more you become adapted to it, it's phenomenal the amount of details you can see.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's nearly what you call Field Grade Nighttime Flight, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The term CAVU is a military pilot acronym for Ceiling and visibility unlimited.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Good thing this is all Field Grade.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's what John and I call Commander's Moon in the navy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got a lot of smart guys here in the CapCom console.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The best I can figure out, we're passing now out of the Ocean of Storms into some more rugged country, here, which is very evident on the surface.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are plotting you right along.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, that engine worked like a champ, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What did you think of those residuals?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We couldn't believe it when you called them down to us.
I know you guys are as happy as clams up there with that performance.
One other thing, we noticed your sleep attitude here yawed out about 20 degrees.
We called for a yaw of zero, and were wondering what we have.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, you got a yaw of 20 degrees because something keeps torquing us over that way.
But is that going to bother you, or do you want it back to zero?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It looks like this water boiler keeps torquing us off, because we haven't noticed that any Apollo's holding inertial anywhere before.
And particularly on this orbital path, you wouldn't expect the yaw to get to you, but it looks like the water boiler is torquing us.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Either that, or there is a big mascon up north or something.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It might be that giant escargot up there.
We'd like you to take it back to zero yaw, and let's start over again.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The steam vent for the glycol/water evaporator (boiler) is positioned between window 1 and the aft heatshield.
Steam vented overboard had a propulsive effect on the spacecraft.
Therefore the yaw motion Young is reporting could have been caused by the prolonged operation of the evaporator.
His alternative theory attributes the yaw to a large mascon (mass concentration).
These are areas of the lunar surface that cause positive gravitational anomalies which alter the spacecraft's orbit.
Mascons are thought to be mostly due to dense mare basaltic lavas, which exhibit a greater than normal gravitational attraction on bodies passing close to them.
However, they should not affect the spacecraft's attitude.
What would affect its attitude is the gravity gradient effect.
Being a long, thin spacecraft of asymmetric mass distribution, the CSM/LM stack is prone to being slowly turned to adopt an upright attitude with its long axis facing the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Boy, this Moon is lit up like a Christmas tree on the dark side.
I don't see any lights, but I mean it is well illuminated from the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm a little behind these other guys; they make...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, say again all about the Christmas tree.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I said I don't mean lit with lights, but it sure is brightly illuminated compared with Earth.
I am a little behind these other two guys.
They make me mind the DSKY.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We recommend you get your share of viewing time, also.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Should be in the area of the crater Grimaldi.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, just to tell you something interesting, it looks like we are coming into the termination of Earthshine here, and we are starting to get long shadows on the hills as we go into the Earth terminator.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Our friendly geologist says that is right, coming up on Earthset here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Would you believe you can even see down in the craters in the Earthshine shadows?
Or is that going a little too far?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It appears - I can recognize, at about 30 south and about 80 west, that big, wide gorge, very rounded at the bottom, that's bordered on one side by the Rook Mountains and on the other side by the Cordillera Mountains.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I can see - I can see Schluter with a central peek very, very easily.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Schluter is a crater at 85 degrees west.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We got 86 percent on the waste-water.
We need a waste-water dump whenever you get to it, and as soon as you can get to it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We can do it right now.
And I'm coming into the sleep attitude at this time.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: What do you want to dump it to, Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got indications that - on the gauge here that we're dumping slowly.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Flight Dynamics Officer, Phil Shaffer, says further tracking shows the orbit as 170.6 by 59.7 nautical miles [315.9 by 110.6 km].
We're still 10 minutes, 45 seconds away from Loss Of Signal on this first revolution of the Moon.
Showing velocity of 5,115 feet per second [1,559 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're coming up on 9 minutes to LOS.
We'll be standing by for your report on the High Gain Antenna on LOS and AOS pitch and yaw positions at AOS of 78:31:21.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, this planet is really something, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's about the only way I know how to put it.
It's got a lot more character than it looks like from sitting down there on the ground.
When you get up close to it, it stands out.
It's got its own features that are certainly clearly recognizable and much different than you see around the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Wish we were there to look at it with you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, there's - There's three lighting conditions very evident.
One is sunlight, Earthshine, and now we're in pitch darkness, although you can still see the lunar horizon against black sky.
It's the black Moon that you can't really see anything on, but there is a definite distinguishable horizon against the black sky where the stars are coming up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And - And both terminators are very interesting.

Terminator produced by sunlight and terminator produced by Earthshine are very similar, although the Earthshine terminator, being of a lower light level, has a very ghost-like, shadowy appearance whereas the sunlight terminators are very definite - definite sharp image - sharp shadow image.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On this - your comment about the - In darkness you can see the Moon horizon; is that just a star dropping or can you actually see features on the horizon?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Charlie, if I had - if I had a pencil I could draw you a - Right across my window, I could draw you a horizon.

There seems to be a - Ever since we went into total darkness on the surface out of Earthshine, there seems to be a continued glow from behind the horizon which lights it up continually, and - You can't distinguish sharp features, but the general terrain you can see.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is that like airglow, or the airglow layer?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Is that glow similar to the airglow layer here on Earth - in Earth orbit?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Hey, I've been - going off and flying the spacecraft, I've just turned around and looked out, and it - stars - You can see it's a bright horizon, but it looks like it might be the Milky Way, but the sky is definitely light, and it goes down and clips off.
You cannot make some of the rough terrain features out about it.
It might be that we could be right close to the Milky Way out there, but it looks like about the same intensity of the Milky Way as you see it at night around the Earth.
It does get lighter over in one section, and we'll give you a comment on that later.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: What's going on out there is no airglow at all; it's just a sharp definition between the Moon surface and this parabolic glow that's out there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's a good sharp horizon there, huh?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I think you could make a cat shot off a horizon like that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can tell it has to be well lighted.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You might make a cat shot, but I'm not.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I didn't even think you knew what that meant.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Oh, I know all the words.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You know, that LM launch might be just like a cat shot.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You can terminate your water dump, and you're looking good as you go over the hill.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: This is a dump of waste (excess) water produced by the fuel cell power generation system.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we've lost the signal as Apollo 10 turns the corner and goes behind the Moon.
The crew giving us a rather graphic description of their first revolution around the Moon.
Shortly after an Acquisition Of Signal on this first rev, you heard Tom Stafford calling out some names of features that heretofore have not been attached to the Moon.
These are unofficial identifications of features that the crew has come up with.
1, the Oklahoma hills, Diamondback Rill, Sidewinder Rill.
Then they have a number of others.
All of these features are primarily along on either side of the ground track in the area leading down to landing sites 2 - in the approach path of landing site 2.
We will acquire Apollo 10 on the second revolution at an elapsed time of 78 hours, 31 minutes, 19 seconds; some 41 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Cernan is setting the HGA pitch and yaw angles using the rotary switches on panel 2.
He is counting through the angle settings on the yaw switch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Positioning the High Gain Antenna Track switch on panel 2 in the Reacq position, places the HGA in the automatic acquisition mode of operation, the antenna will perform as in the Track Auto mode, pointing towards the center of the Earth, provided it is within 60° of the antennas boresight axis.
Outside these scan limits it would automatically switch to Man (manual) mode to return to within the scan limits.
The antenna will remain in manual mode until it has arrived at an indicated Pitch/Yaw position and a signal was present.
With the High Gain Antenna positioned at manually preset positions, and with a signal present, the mode of operation reverts automatically to Track Auto mode of operation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The predicted sunrise time read up to the crew was 77:51:40.
Now that sunlight is streaming through the windows, the panel lights need to be brought to full brightness.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are discussing the remaining propellant quantities following the largest planned SPS burn, LOI-1.
The quantities displayed on the onboard gauges varied depending on the pressure and temperature in the propellants tanks.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Gene is applying the extra 10 minutes due to their delayed arrival in lunar orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With ORDEAL panel 13 in Orb Rate mode, the spacecraft will maintain the same attitude with respect to the surface it is orbiting, in this case the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 77:58:27 is the predicted time for passing the 150°W meridian.
At this time the spacecraft attitude is held at yaw 0°, roll 135°, pitch 208°.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At the time of Apollo 10's mission, many features on the lunar far side were yet to be given formal names by the IAU (International Astronomical Union).
On the maps being referred to by the crew many of the unnamed craters were given a numeric ID and large basins or Mare were given a Roman numeral ID.
Modern names have been inserted when they have been adopted.
Basin XV was named America by the crew but is now known as Korolev centred at 4°S, 158°W.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is down in the Lower Equipment Bay trying to sight the lunar surface through the navigation optics.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10 is now crossing the 180° longitude line of the far side.
Crater 308 is now known as Daedalus and is centred at 6°S, 180°E, 309 is Racah centred at 14°S, 179°W.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew which, two of which flew together on Gemini IX-A (Stafford and Cernan) have slightly amended the official nomenclature for basin IX which is now known as the crater Mendeleev, centred at 142°E, 6°N.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 310 is now known as Icarus, centred at 6°S, 173°W.
Icarus has a very pronounced central peak.
It was filmed in 16-mm on magazine F.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 308 is now known as Daedalus and is centred at 6°S, 180°E
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 221 is now known as Papaleski, centred at 11°N, 164°E, 223 is Spencer Jones centred at 13°N, 166°E.
Both can be seen in the 70mm photograph, AS10-34-5170.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 225 is now known as Coriolis, centred at 1°N, 172°E and shown in photograph AS10-28-4067.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CP-1 is a landmark track target that was tracked optically using either the sextant or the scanning telescope in the Command Module.
This tracking was carried out on several missions on a variety if targets near the spacecraft's groundtrack.
These landmark locations can be used as control points for extending selenodetic control to the lunar far side.
CP-1 was situated on the far side at 0.86°N, 170.15°E.
See location on chart at the beginning of thesection.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 224 is now known as Anderson, centred at 16°N, 171°E.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 223 is now known as Spencer Jones centred at 13°N, 166°E, 221 as Papaleski, centred at 11°N, 164°E, see photographs AS10-31-4653 and AS10-31-4654.
Crater 220 is now known as Mandel'shtam A, centred at 5°N, 163°E.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Basin IX is now known as the crater Mendeleev, centred at 142°E, 6°N.
It is shown in the following photographs from the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CP-2 is another landmark tracking target, located at 0.58°N, 127.59°E.
See location on chart at the beginning of thesection.
It is also visible on AS10-34-5109.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 211 is now known as King, centred at 5°N, 120°E.
It is shown in the following photographs from the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 208 is Guyot, centred at 11°N, 117°E, and 210 is Kostinskiy, centred at 15°N, 119°E.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 78 hours, 29 minutes.
We're about 2 minutes away from acquisition of Apollo 10 on its second lunar revolution.
Shortly after acquiring with spacecraft, the ground will give the crew a Go/No-go for the LOI-2 burn.
This is the maneuver designed to circularize the orbit to as near to 60 nautical miles [111 km] as possible.
This maneuver will take place behind the Moon as did the Lunar Orbit Insertion number 1 burn.
It will come at approximately 80 hours, 22 minutes.
We'll get the precise time when they pass up the update.
It'll be a much shorter duration burn but again with the Service Propulsion System.
We're less than a minute now from acquisition time.
We'll stand by live for first transmission from Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Looks like the Reacq mode works pretty well there, and - everything - We've acquired, and read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We had you - Go through that again about the Reacq.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I said I went through those pitch and yaw angles in the Reacq.
Narrow deadband in this attitude picks you up loud and strong, here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Where did the antenna go to in pitch and yaw at LOS?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, I'm not sure I can answer that one specific question.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got a beauty coming up.
There's a little bit of color on this son of a bitch.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Do you think the antenna went to the angles that you had dialled in, or fairly close to it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It went to those angles at LOS.
I went to Reacq and fired LOS, and it did go to those angles, and that's where it stayed and picked you up on the way back.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is confirming that the HGA in Reacq (reacquire) mode, was set to the pointing angles manually that had been specified and locked on to the ground signal without any further intervention by the crew at AOS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're waiting for high bit rate telemetry so we can command state vector and some target PADs.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At 078:30:00 the Flight Plan dictates the spacecraft should be maneuvered to roll 180°, pitch 208°, yaw 000° to facilitate crew observations of the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The SC Cont (spacecraft control) switch on panel 1 would be placed in the CMC position, so that the computer is controlling the attitude and attitude rates.
The CMC Mode switch, also on panel 1, in the Free position, allows external inputs (discretes) to the CMC such as from the translation and rotation hand controllers.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are having trouble getting our high bit rate command in.
We'd like you to select high bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: [MCC-H are unable to switch the HGA to High Bit Rate remotely and are asking the crew to switch over onboard, using the PCM Bit Rate switch on panel 3.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I gave it to you, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got a load for you, and if you'll give us the computer and Accept, we'll send you up a maneuver PAD - correction - a target load and a state vector.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we have a LOI-2 PAD, a TEI PAD, and a map update for you, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, give me the map update first, would you, please?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's Rev 3, and we got LOS time of 79:56:22, 80:06:41, AOS 80:40:45.
Got a sunrise of 80:00:13, and a sunset of 81:14:30.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Sun rises at 80:00:13 and sets at 81:14:30.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Map update times for rev 3; LOS, 79:56:22; sunrise, 80:00:13; crossing 150°W, 80:06:41; AOS, 80:40:45; sunset, 81:14:30.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like you to go Up Telemetry, Command Reset to Command Reset and back to Normal.
We're having trouble getting our commands in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MCC-H are having trouble uplinking commands and are requesting the crew to move the Up Telemetry, Command Reset switch on panel 3 from Normal to Command Reset and then back to Normal.
This switch is spring loaded so it automatically returns to the center-Normal position.
In the Command Reset position, performs a real-time command reset function and keeps power applied to the power supply.
This resets all real time command relays except those relays affecting the system A abort light and the crew alarm.
so the affected equipment will resume the operational mode dictated by their control switches on the panel 3.
Effectively it reboots the real time command relays so that any errors should be removed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And if you're ready to copy, I have your LOI-2 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now we'd like to Up the Telemetry Command Reset to Off and then back to Normal.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Off and then Normal, and I'm ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Moving the Up Telemetry switch on panel 3 to the Off position removes power from the up data link equipment.
The momentary removal of power that MCC-H is requesting is an attempt to reset the real time command relays enabling uplink data transmission and commands.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: SPS/G&N: 38650; plus 1.83, minus 0.74; 080:25:07.38; Noun 81 is minus 0139.0, plus all balls, minus all balls; 000, 209, 000; and Noun 44 is 00601, plus 00601; 0139.0, 0:14, 0132.5; sextant star, 16, 220.5 23.2.
Rest of the PAD is NA.
Your set stars are Vega and Deneb; 241, 240, 013.
Two jets at 17 seconds on the ullage.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: LOI-2, SPS/G&N: 38650; plus 1.83, minus 0.74; 080:25:07.38; minus 0139.0, plus all balls, minus all balls; roll 000, pitch is 209, yaw is 000; 0060.1, plus 0060.1; 0139.0, 0:14, 132.5; sextant star is 16, 220.5, 23.2; Vega and Deneb, 241, 240, 013.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: May have read it back wrong.
That's what I've got written down, 01325.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: An interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD is gives the parameters for Lunar Orbit Insertion burn 2.

The LOI-1 burn brought them into lunar orbit, however it was designed to be slightly shorter than required for a circular orbit so that any errors that would risk causing the spacecraft to impact the Moon would be reduced and a slightly longer SPS over-run could be tolerated.

LOI-2 is a relatively small burn that finally circularises their orbit for the remainder of the lunar stay.Systems:The burn will be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):38,650 pounds (17,571 kg).
The spacecraft weight has dropped considerably due to the propellant used for the LOI-1 burn.Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+1.83° and -0.74°.
These are the angles through which the SPS engine should be gimbaled (swivelled) to ensure that, on ignition, its thrust acts through the spacecraft's centre of gravity.
During the burn, the G&N system will swivel the engine as necessary to maintain this condition.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):74 hours, 08 minutes, 7.38 seconds.

The burn will occur above the far side as the spacecraft reaches its pericynthion, the lowest point in its orbit.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, -139.0 (-42.4); y and z components are both zero.

Since the change in velocity is expressed relative to the Local Vertical/Local Horizontal frame of reference, we can see that this burn is a pure retrograde burn and therefore meant to slow the spacecraft down with respect to the Moon.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 0°; Pitch, 209°; Yaw, 0°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.
The current alignment of the platform was meant to match the spacecraft's prograde attitude for this burn when it was calculated such that the burn attitude would be 0°, 180°, 0°.
This is to make it easier to monitor the attitude during what is an especially critical burn (one which, if erroneous, could cause impact.

The fact that the pitch and yaw are slightly different to the ideal shows that small dispersions are being compensated for by this burn.HA, expected apocynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):60.1 nautical miles (111.3km).HP, expected pericynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):60.1 nautical miles (111.3 km).The apocynthion and pericynthion are essentially identical showing that a circular orbit is being aimed for.Delta-VT:139.0 fps (42.4 m/s).
This is the total change in velocity the spacecraft would experience.

(It is a vector sum of the three components given above though since two of them are zero, it equals the minus-X component in magnitude.)Burn duration or burn time:14 seconds.Delta-VC:132.5 fps (40.4 m/s).
This value is entered into the Delta-V counter of the EMS (Entry Monitor System) panel.
This figure will descend towards zero as the engine burns.

If the Guidance and Control System fails to stop the burn, the EMS will do so but it has to be given a low Delta-V figure to take account of the engine's tail-off thrust after shutdown.Sextant star:Star 16 (Procyon) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 220.5° and 23.2° respectively.
This is part of an attitude check.Other parameters on the PAD sheet are not applicable to this manoeuvre.
This includes a second star check that would be made using the COAS.

However, as this device has to be used through the windows and these are facing towards the Moon, it is irrelevant for this burn.GDC Align stars:Stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Vega and Deneb.GDC Align angles:The align angles are roll, 241°; pitch, 240°; yaw, 13°.The final note is that the ullage burn to settle the propellants in their tanks of 17 seconds duration using two rearward facing SM RCS jets.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And stand by for the TEI PAD.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We have your primary Evap dried out.
We'd like you to close the backpressure valve.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The glycol/water evaporator has dried out as occurred during launch.

MCC-H has requested that the Steam Press Auto/Man switch be placed in Man, which allows manual control of the steam pressure control valve, and the Steam Press Incr/Decr switch be placed in Incr, which applies AC power to the steam control valve, which moves the valve in the closed direction and allows the steam pressure in the evaporator to increase.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It appears we are having a little problem with our ground uplinking capability.
I have a TEI-5 PAD, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: My finger is still on a button here; I'll be right with you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead, Charlie, with the TEI PAD.
It's been more than 58 seconds, but...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Steam Press Incr switch on panel 2 takes 58 seconds from open to completely closed, so Cernan is having to hold the switch in the Incr position for at least this length of time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by for about a minute.
We are going to bring down our uplink, and you won't hear us for about a minute.
We're going to try to reconfigure ground site.
We've got problems with our uplink.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The time for the lunar orbit insertion No.
Duration of the burn will be 14 seconds.

For the first time, we will be using ullage prior to this burn using two of the RCS thrusters, burning those for 17 seconds prior to the service propulsion system ignition to settle the propellants in the tank.
This is necessary after that long LOI No.
1 burn, we have a large ullage volume in the tank.
Not near as many, as much propellants as before and we will use ullage to settle those propellants before igniting the SPS.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston with the TEI-5 PAD, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: TEI-5, SPS/G&N: minus 061, plus 0 - correction - the Noun 47 is NA.
Starting off with Noun 48: minus 0.61, plus 0.47; 086:19:10.00; plus 3643.0, minus 0149.3, plus 0054.6; pitch angle is 025.
Rest of the PAD is NA.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: TEI-5, SPS/G&N: starting with Noun 48 is minus 0.61, plus 0.47; 086:19:10.00; plus 3643.0, minus 0149.3, plus 0054.6; and pitch angle is 025.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a revised version for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev 5.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Normally the spacecraft weight is given at this point but it is being omitted in this simplified abort PAD.Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.61° and +0.47°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):86 hours, 19 minutes, 10.00 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,643.0 (+1,110.6); Y, +149.3 (+45.5); Z, -54.6 (-16.6).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, NA; Pitch, 25°.All the subsequent items on the form are not applicable to this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we've had a problem with our uplink at Goldstone.
They are configuring now, and we'll be with you in a load momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If this next burn goes as planned, we will have circularized the orbit at 60.1 nautical miles [111.3 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 10, while we got a moment here, before we get our load into you, we've got a couple of comments.
For LOI-2, we recommend you just place the oxidizer flow increase valve to Normal, and go Primary.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You want me to stay Normal in Primary through that whole burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: MCC-H is instructing the crew to commence the LOI-2 SPS burn with the Prim (primary) propellant utilization valve selected and the Oxid Flow Valve switch in the Norm position.
When the Oxid Flow Valve switch is in Norm and the Oxid Flow Valve select switch is in Prim, the sliding gate valves are in a nominal flow position.
The upper and lower Oxid Flow Valve position indicators are gray.
Cernan now has clarification on how to proceed with managing the propellant balancing during LOI-2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We feel that it's so short that that's the best position, and then for TEI we'll have a story for you on how we want you to operate the PUGS.
Also for TV, if we try the dark side TV, recommend a ALC to INSIDE and an f-stop of 2.2.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got that, Charlie, and I want you to understand now that I did go back to Normal about 20 seconds before the LOI-1 burn ended, so that's where I am, and after I did that I went - my increase unbalance moved up probably about 100 pounds [45 kg].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Charlie, just for reference as to exactly where we are, we're looking right down on the top of Messier and Messier A, and we'll be - We've got Taruntius to one side, and we're just right in the middle of the Sea of Fertility coming right up the track into the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can really see some boulders in the bottom of Messier A, now.
You can see some tremendous boulders down there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're coming up with the load now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're in the CMC and Accept and P00.
We can see the load coming.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You guys been taking some good pictures for us?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, for correlation, I've been on the same sites.
I've been shooting one black and white of the special part of it.
Shooting one black and white and one of the special color on the same reference.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We think we've got a few colors here for you.
At least you're certain of the ones that are real black, going into whites, and then some browns.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We heard your tape on the back side during the LOI-1, and seemed like there was a disagreement between brownish and bluish, there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The blue was just a little remark.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you've got a moment to comment, on your tape playback from LOI-1 after the burn was completed, we heard a comment about "Hey, look at that bubble." Could you elaborate on that?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, I guess it was a bubble of water or something, right - hanging right with us after the burn.
I tried to take a picture of it.
I don't know if we got it or not, but it was a bluish, crystalline-type bubble about - well, about 5 feet out where the LM thrusters are.
It probably came either from water or from the resultant residual of the SPS burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, maybe a half an inch in diameter.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This time we are looking down, right down on B-1 out of the hatch window.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: How's the terrain look around there?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How does the terrain look around that area?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: With the naked eye, I'd say it's full of holes.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It looks pretty smooth on our map.
Of course, we got one of the world here, but it's real smooth.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, through the monocular, you can see little shiny fresh craters that you can't see with the naked eye.
They're pretty well scattered, but there's quite a few of them down there that you can't seem to pick out with the naked eye.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can go back to Block.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're black in - back in Block.
We're passing right over Maskelyne, now, and John is shooting back at Site 1.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: At this rate, we're going to run out of all our film in a couple of revs.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: A slight shudder emanated from the geologist back there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, I'm personally amazed how accurate the maps are at picking out these landmarks and craters.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can put your PCM switch back to Low Bit Rate.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MCC-H had been having problems with uplinking telemetry and commands, so had to ask the crew to select High Bit Rate manually at078:38:59.
They are now asking for Low Bit Rate to be manually selected.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We can now see quite a bit more of Moltke as the Moon revolves, and Landing Site 2 is rough.
It's just barely starting to come in.
It's still too early to tell much about it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 10, can you - can you see the - on the CSM lunar orbit map - the spot marked 112 near Site 2?
It looks like a bright, whitish crater.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Moltke is very, very easy to see.
We've seen it both times around.
Not only are the rilles, but the low ridges are very distinguishable approaching the landing sites.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Sun angle is such that we cannot see into the bottom of the - of Diamondback and Sidewinder rilles.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Flight Surgeon is taking a look at the heart rate data now from the playback during the LOI-1 burn.
The ignition time of that burn was 75:55:53.
At 75:55:50 Tom Stafford's heart rate was 120.
At 75:56:10 was 123, at 75:56:30, it was back to 120.
For the Command Module Pilot, John Young at those same times it was 94, 98, 83 and 86.
And for Lunar Module Pilot Gene Cernan it was 91, 85, 74 and 70.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 now near the landing site 3 area.
This is also the area where Surveyor 6 landed.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We see the solar corona, and it's really beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: What time and how long we can, see it now.
The Sun went down exactly at about 45, and we can still see edges of it.
It's mostly a long shaft of light - and we can still see it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's - The corona is still out there.
You can see it quite bigger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And you can see stars within about - I can see some at - about 20 degrees of the corona.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: On the TV pass, do you think we could pick that up?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Shouldn't be any problem in this attitude, if we're in this attitude upside down going away.
And we'll go from interior to the out, there.
You should see it; it's a long streak, and right now it's finally started to fade out, Charlie.
It was in a period of nearly 2 minutes that we could see it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 10, how long does the shafting look as it comes across?
Does it get shorter as you go away, or just sort of fade out?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It just fades out, and the shafting's getting shorter and shorter.
There's just a little bit left there, and it'll be gone in a few seconds.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, and it spread over an area - a very small area of the horizon - just right in the vicinity where sun sets.
It doesn't go any further either left or right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Still - still see traces of it.
It's greatly diminished, now, but you can still see a few traces of it.
Okay, we'll get on with our P52.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Most of that description was by Tom Stafford with Gene Cernan coming in right there at the- along toward the last.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have about 36 minutes left before Loss Of Signal on this revolution number 2.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 is now realigning their inertial measurement unit and shortly should be maneuvering into the attitude for the Lunar Orbit Insertion number 2 burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 is - has just passed the 45-degree west longitude line.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like to leave your backpressure valve closed for another rev or so, look at it.
We'll probably go to sleep tonight with it closed.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: With the water/glycol evaporator having dried out for the second time in the mission, MCC-H is asking the crew to leave theSteam Press switches in Man and Decrwhilst they continue to troubleshoot the issue.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've lost lock on the High Gain Antenna that was the cause of all that noise.
It happened when the spacecraft started maneuvering to the burn attitude.
We're apparently on the Omnis now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 is 22 minutes away from Loss Of Signal on the second revolution coming up on the 60 degree west longitude line in the Ocean of Storms area.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: If you read, we'd like you to select Omni Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you aren't already on Omni Charlie, we'd like you to select Omni Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 10, we're reading you about three-by, how me?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now I'm reading you loud and clear.
We lost you on High Gain.
I've been waiting to pick you up on Omni.
We're maneuvering out of the burn attitude.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't know whether you copied my last transmission before we broke lock.
We're going to leave the backpressure valve closed and watch it for another rev; and probably for the sleep configuration, we'll have it off.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you recommend an Omni for the burn?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: For the burn attitude before LOS.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's where I am now, so I'll stay there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: On the last pass on the back side, we're pretty sure we identified through the optics going backwards, CP-1 and CP-2.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: 10, did you call it up or did you just manually track?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Just manually tracked; if we'd have called it up, that would have shot our wide deadband out of the sky.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If the crew had requested the CMC to assist in pointing the CM optics at landmark targets CP-1 & CP-2, the maneuver would have contravened the deadband they are currently set in.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That was John Young reporting they could see the selected land marks on the back side of the Moon.
Flight Director Gerry Griffin is taking a status check here.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hey, Houston, maybe it wouldn't hurt the wide deadband.
Would you check on that for us?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If you call up any program, it'll collapse the deadband back down.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's what I was afraid of.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm looking ahead in the Flight Plan to 81 hours when we really start getting busy, there.
One thing we're going to want to do is we want to delay the canister change and also that fuel cell O2purge until afterwards.
The CO2content's real low and delaying it an hour or two isn't going to hurt a thing.

And we want to get through that busy time without any interruptions so we're recommending delaying there at 81:20 the canister change and the fuel cell O2at 81:40 until after we get through most of this activity.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew workload at approximately 80:00 GET includes a P52 IMU realignment, pressurisation of the LM and entry of the LM for inspection and initial configuration.
Stafford is using his commander prerogative to delay some minor Flight Plan items such as the changing the lithium hydroxide CO2filters and fuel cell purges.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have 10 minutes of acquisition time left in this pass and we're 39 minutes away from the LOI 2 burn.
This will be another retrograde burn with the spacecraft pitched up 10 degrees.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Do you want us to go into 40 before we lose you at LOS?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'd like to see a P40, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The prime function of Program 40 is to have the computer control the SPS engine throughout a burn.
It will start and stop the engine and display the effect of the burn on the DSKY.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: P40 is the Service Propulsion System testing program in the Command Module Computer.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are you all getting high bit rate now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got you low bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: In other words, you can read all that stuff; you just need high bit rate to uplink it, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 10, we can command the low bit rate even, but it takes a little hit longer.
We got some parameters set on our low bit rate, but all your DSKY stuff we can see low bit rate.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Coming up on 5 minutes to LOS.
You're looking good going on over the hill.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And 10, one more update for you: after your maneuver - after LOI-2 as we come around the horn, the High Gain Antenna for the comm will be a pitch of a minus 55.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MCC-H is refining the HGA pointing angle in pitch to 55°, at AOS.
The 148° yaw angle specified in the Flight Plan remains correct.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 79 hours, 55 minutes.
We're still showing 40 seconds to Loss Of Signal, but the noise has stopped.
It appeared we have broken a lock there.
We've got about 30 seconds left till the Loss Of Signal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And we do have Loss Of Signal now.
We're 28 minutes, 30 seconds away from Lunar Orbit Insertion number 2 burn.
The maneuver by which we will circularize Apollo 10's orbit.
We've targeted for a circular orbit of 60.1 nautical miles [111.3 km].
We're presently showing Apollo 10 in a lunar orbit of 170.7 by 60.1 nautical miles [316.1 by 111.3 km].
A review briefly, this upcoming burn, it will be Service Propulsion System, retrograde with the spacecraft pitched up 10 degrees.

Ignition time is 80 hours, 25 minutes, 7 seconds while Apollo 10 is behind the Moon and out of contact with the Earth, we will have to await Acquisition Of Signal and a post-burn report from the crew to find out how it went.
A Delta-V of 139 feet per second, duration of the burn 14 seconds.
We will next acquire Apollo 10 at 80 hours, 40 minutes, 47 seconds; 42 minutes and 37 seconds from now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: CDH-Constant Differential (Delta) Height manoeuvre will place the LM on a coelliptic orbit 15 nm below that of the CSM during the rendezvous manoeuvres in lunar orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CSI - Concentric Sequence Initiation manoeuvre places the LM in a orbit of similar shape to that of the CSM, but lower, during the rendezvous manoeuvres in lunar orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When the crew make their first foray into the LM 'Snoopy', later on day 4, they will perform some housekeeping chores and transfer some equipment from the CSM.

Amongst the equipment to be transferred are:Radiator survey meter.16 mm film (6 magazines in 1 bag).70 mm film (3 magazines in 1 bag).Monocular.Flight data file items.The housekeeping chores to be performed, include:Unsnap helmet stowage bags & stow on cabin floor.Unstow mirror, checklist, disposal assembly and configure for use.Stow ISA over PLSS recharge station.Unstow 70mm & 16mm cameras and configure for use.Stow crew log.The crew are aware that all this work is likely to ensure a late finish to the day's proceedings.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
